title: Built-in Functions
primer:
Each shading language ships with a large standard library of GPU-accelerated built-in functions. Most math is the same across GLSL, HLSL, and Slang — but names and signatures diverge just enough to cause bugs when porting.

The most common gotchas:

  • lerp vs mix — same semantics, different name (HLSL/Slang vs GLSL).
  • saturate vs clamp — HLSL's saturate(x) is equivalent to GLSL's clamp(x, 0.0, 1.0); GLSL has no saturate.
  • frac vs fract — fractional part of a float (HLSL vs GLSL).
  • ddx/ddy vs dFdx/dFdy — screen-space partial derivatives (HLSL vs GLSL).
  • mul(M, v) vs M * v — HLSL requires the explicit mul() function for matrix-vector multiply; GLSL uses the * operator directly.
  • atan2 vs atan — two-argument arctangent is atan2(y,x) in HLSL/Slang but atan(y,x) in GLSL.

In this lesson you will fix broken HLSL-in-GLSL translation errors and practice finding the correct Slang equivalents.

challenges.title: Fix broken HLSL→GLSL translation
hints: frac→fract, saturate(x)→clamp(x, 0.0, 1.0), lerp→mix. That covers four of the five errors.
atan2(y, x) in HLSL becomes atan(y, x) in GLSL — GLSL's two-argument atan is the same function.
challenges.unlocks_theory: The core rename table for built-ins: frac→fract, saturate(x)→clamp(x,0.0,1.0), lerp→mix, atan2(y,x)→atan(y,x), ddx→dFdx, ddy→dFdy, mul(M,v)→M*v. Functions that do NOT need renaming: sin, cos, tan, sqrt, pow, abs, floor, ceil, round, step, smoothstep, length, dot, cross, normalize, reflect, refract.

hlsl.note: Correct HLSL version using native HLSL built-ins.

slang.note: Slang uses HLSL built-in names; it also exposes mix() as an alias for lerp().

challenges.title: Matrix multiply: mul() vs *
challenges.description:
In HLSL, matrix-vector multiplication requires the explicit mul() function. In GLSL, the * operator handles it directly. The difference shows up clearly when transforming positions or applying rotation matrices.

HLSL reference:
```hlsl
float4x4 mvp;   // model-view-projection matrix
float4 pos;     // homogeneous vertex position
float4 clip = mul(mvp, pos);
```

In GLSL, write a fragment shader that applies a 2D rotation matrix to the UV coordinates using `*` (not mul). Construct a 2x2 rotation matrix from `u_time` and multiply it against `v_uv - 0.5` to spin the UV space. Output the rotated UV as a color.

Key differences:
- HLSL uses `mul(matrix, vector)` — * is element-wise for matrices in HLSL.
- GLSL uses `matrix * vector` directly — * is proper linear algebra.
- Slang supports both.
hints: GLSL overloads * for matrix-vector products — just write rot * centered. No mul() needed.
HLSL's mul(M, v) and GLSL's M * v produce the same result for column-major matrices.
challenges.unlocks_theory: HLSL requires mul(matrix, vector) because * is element-wise by default for matrices. GLSL defines * as proper linear-algebraic multiplication for mat*vec, vec*mat, and mat*mat — making mul() unnecessary. Slang supports both mul() and * for compatibility; when targeting GLSL output, the Slang compiler emits *. This difference trips up HLSL developers porting to GLSL: forgetting mul() and using * in HLSL gives silent element-wise wrong results, while using mul() in GLSL is a compile error.

hlsl.note: HLSL pixel shader using mul() for matrix-vector multiply.

slang.note: Slang accepts both mul() and * — mul() shown here for HLSL compatibility.

challenges.title: Screen-space derivatives: ddx/ddy → dFdx/dFdy
hints: ddx(v) in HLSL is dFdx(v) in GLSL — measures how v changes horizontally across a 2x2 pixel quad.
ddy(v) in HLSL is dFdy(v) in GLSL. Note: GLSL's dFdy may flip sign on some drivers vs HLSL's ddy.
challenges.unlocks_theory: Partial derivatives (dFdx/dFdy in GLSL, ddx/ddy in HLSL) are computed at no cost by comparing values across the 2x2 pixel quads the GPU processes in parallel. They are essential for anti-aliased procedural geometry, automatic mip-map selection in texture2D(), and anisotropic filtering. Slang exposes both ddx()/ddy() and dFdx()/dFdy() aliases — when targeting GLSL it emits dFdx/dFdy.

hlsl.note: Original HLSL grid shader using ddx/ddy.

slang.note: Slang accepts ddx/ddy; when cross-compiled to GLSL the compiler emits dFdx/dFdy.
